video_title: Trinity Wall Street's Nicholas Csicsko: How Transparency Survives a 50% Drawdown
transcript: [music] Nick, thanks so much for doing this. >> Thanks for having me. >> It is very customary of a capital allocators interview to start with your background and I'm especially excited to dive into this subject with you because it's rare that someone finds their way into the investment industry from music. So why don't you share how you got into this industry in the first place? Music brought me to New York to study uh at the Giuliard School. And during my masters, I was studying composition and trying to make ends meet, working a lot of jobs. And then all of a sudden, I stayed and joined the doctoral program at Jiuliard. And it was free. And the doctoral program came with a stipend. And all of a sudden, I didn't know what to do with that stipend. And so, cuz I was working and paying rent and doing the things people do. And so I put it in a brokerage account and um just started buying stocks and it was a whole experience and um it was 2007 and so I felt smart for a little bit and then 2008 happened and I felt uh the opposite but it was certainly volatile and I fell in love and I didn't know why but I wanted to figure things out and so I started reading and listening to everything I could and that just drove me to try to become an investor and so then I talked my way into Giuliard, this endowment's uh investment office. And so I was their first and only intern. At the time it was in-house. It's since been outsourced. And uh the gentleman there at the time really gave me a shot and I started to learn about this whole job. And then that ended up really through a bunch of steps meeting uh my first real mentor and um a gentleman named Bill Peterson who was at the time the CIO of the Sloan Foundation uh let me have a shot. And so I joined the Sloan Foundation and um kind of just took it from there. >> What was it about investing in stocks in 2007 that prompted you to say, I actually want to pivot my career interests to investing as opposed to music. >> I was entered into this different world of trying to figure out why businesses do what they do and then how that applies to economies as a whole and and and countries and geopolitics. And it was just fascinating. And so I've always kind of wanted to just keep learning and it turns out that investing is one of the best ways to learn about kind of everything. And so I got hooked. >> How did you talk your way into an inter internship? What did you say? >> I don't remember, but I remember the moment I got the call and I did one of those cliche fist pumps to myself cuz I really felt like I'd accomplished unpaid internship was real something. And um I think just at the time candidly I was in the system. I was already student teaching. I met the background checks to to be in the building. And so they said if this doesn't work in a week we can just rotate down to one of the MBAs that's applied because uh the job market was tough. And then so I just grinded and uh they let me stick around. What lessons did you learn from your time in music that you've been able to take to your career in investing? >> I would highlight three quickly is that relationships matter. And so when you're doing anything that involved in music, you're working with ensembles and you're you're you have different components. You might be the composer. You you're going to then work with the ensemble and then you're going to have to deal with an audience. So you have all these different stakeholders and your relationship with all of them becomes critical in terms of the output. And so from day one I realized that truly everything is relationship business and and certainly music is this other element is a bit trickier but every great composer is balancing the horizontal the melody the line of the music and the vertical which is the composite what it creates the harmony. And I think about that between managers and portfolios and the best composers make the least amount of sacrifices or the most graceful things and the compromises they make on the horizontal or the melody to make the harmony work. You often hear the top and the bottom of a piece of music, the melody and the bass, but that stuff in the middle is really what drives great music. And that's also in my mind what drives a great portfolio is not losing sight of that. And then the last thing is pretty basic, but if you're going to be a musician, if you're going to be out there, everyone senses if you're being authentic. Everyone senses if you're being true to yourself because that just comes through. And when it is, people open up and people say, "Oh, that was great." You know, you touch someone and and then they react. And so I think that the best investors I meet know themselves and they spend their whole life trying to be that authentic version. And it's really hard with investing because you get all this feedback, positive and negative, and all this noise. And the whole time you're just trying to figure out what kind of investor I am, who am I? And not lose sight of that. >> You meet your mentor, you join the Sloan Foundation. What were you focused on earlier in your career? an investment meeting for someone who doesn't speak the language is like sitting through uh a foreign language class and things, you know, really uh go by that you miss. And so as you start to come up the curve, things slow down and you really start to understand what's going on. And so I just had great people around me that were always willing to talk me through things. and some of my best friends in the industry today I met around that time and I still remember folks taking me through what a DCF is. You know, I had endless questions of why people own gold and you know, there's a sitting CIO who knows who he is who's just been really kind to me through all these moments uh of my development in life that's just kind of been there. And so, uh, I think I was just fortunate enough to stumble into some great relationships and folks that really helped me. It's great. >> What prompted then the move to Trinity? >> So, I'd been at Sloan for almost 5 years and uh, had the opportunity to meet Meredith Jenkins and a bunch of other folks uh, in the industry and she happened to be building a team at Trinity. And Trinity was this new endowment inside of a 320-year-old institution. and um she took a shot on me, I took a shot on her and we started really building Trinity out in 2016. >> What was that like joining an organization that didn't have an established investment office? >> We had to figure a lot of things out. We had to set an internal culture, decide what we wanted to be, start to meet the world, and say we existed. And Trinity has a pretty unique story, so it was a fun story to tell. I had previously seen National Treasure with Nicholas Cage and that was my conception of where Trinity's origin story was and and assets and it was a bit off but you know it was it was it was fun from day one and it was um just even more learning and you know we've ramped from then and it's just been a decade of fun. >> What were some of the tenants of the culture that you wanted to create at that time? Trinity as a whole is a very warming, welcoming place. And that's certainly the ethos of of Meredith. And so I'm not sure I was setting it, I was following. And uh I embraced it from day one. We started with that and we started with a lot of relationships we had and and folks that we could trust to get going because we really had a pool of capital to get put to work right away. We did inherit a portfolio that was fully invested. So it was a combination of getting to know that and then rotating into things that we felt fit our strategy better. >> What were the tenants of the portfolio and the portfolio strategy that you wanted to establish even though you were inheriting a new portfolio? Well, Trinity has a lot of stakeholders and so the first thing we really dealt with was trying to understand their needs, their concerns and meeting them where they were. And our first line of defense was let's know what we own and let's really know who we partner with. And um that really skewed us towards fundamental managers. We certainly have diversifiers that that have very different approaches and and some quantitative and systematic strategies, but by and large it pushed us towards managers that were transparent and managers that really often concentrated in specific holdings, all to be able to more easily speak to our stakeholders as to where the capital was and in what kind of businesses um Trinity invested. And so why don't we dig into some of those core tenants that you mentioned, transparency being the one that you called out as a top priority for a pool like yours. What does that mean and what does that look like from your seat? >> I'd start with the disclaimer that everyone has their own style and I really don't think they're right ways. There's just a lot of wrong ways, if you will. And and with the base condition that, you know, investing is hard and luck and skill plays a role in everything everyone does every day. And I'm convinced that every investor will have rough patches. And so the basic condition and idea is that you want to prepare for those moments in advance. And so if relationships are always going to be tested, then we want to behave and we certainly look for our partners to behave with the mindset of being prepared for those moments. So that if you imagine yourself in your worst moment, that's how you behave in the good times. And it serves as this baseline for for how you want to communicate, how you want to treat your partners, just how you want to be. And so in that case, transparency builds stability and duration into relationships. And it's really tested during periods of stress. So the normal reaction for people when they're challenged is to put their guard up, is to become defensive. And a lot of times I've found that the opposite is actually most successful in investing. And if you let someone in and they understand where you're coming from, what you're struggling with, the decisions you're you're thinking to make, you can have a lot more success. And so, in that way, all of a sudden, capital stops being funible because it's no longer just money. It's a partnership. And I want to be clear that this is a two-way street. It's not just that we look for our partners to be great. We want to be great to our partners. And so if we can be perceived as a top quartortile or potentially even top desile partner, we will reap those rewards over time, access to to the manager themselves or just unique opportunities that present themselves along the way. I give you an example of a fund that I would call just an old school long short fund. uh this manager really just cares about investing, loves shorting, has an innate ability to short, and has really made a lot of money shorting over the years. And so, this manager started their business in 2008, and they've had a great long-term track record. But I would say that some people have just periods of meh and other people have real periods of stress. And so, you know, we invested with this manager in 2017 and the first few years were good because I'd known that manager for a long time. We had a pretty high level of transparency. You know, every fund is going to be different. Every fund's ability to share what they're doing, what they own, how they own it is going to change over time and it's going to be bespoke. But this manager specifically was very open about sharing his whole portfolio to to the extent that he he would just he still does just sends uh his entire portfolio in an Excel file to to me monthly because you know he he's doesn't really have any concerns about what we would do with that information. This manager is the type of manager that closed outside capital when he was about at 300 million in assets because he didn't ever really want to be over 5600 million and had that logic that's not there with most folks that is, hey, I should close about half of my capacity because I want to double the money before I'm returning capital. And so this was all good. The setup was good, but 2019 was this manager's worst year in the firm's history. And they were down 14%. And so we spent a lot of time that year talking about what was going on and you know the short book was really getting tested and and it was challenging him in really extreme ways. And so fund was down 14. That's not the world's worst year. And so coming into 2020 we added to this position. And I still remember he came to our office and you know we we came out of that saying this is the time to add and it wasn't the time to add. So 2020 was this manager's worst year and and he was down an additional 30%. So we thought 2019 was the worst year, but it turns out 2020 was the worst year so far. And so that led to a real moment of soularching. You know, you have a draw down that's starting to get close to 50% at the trough. [gasps] And so over the course of that year, you know, I I went back and spent a lot of time with that manager. I think I met with them at least seven or eight times. And this manager works out of Dallas and very responsive but really is best to meet in person. No IRBD function, nothing like that. And it was co and it just happened to be that this manager got kicked out of his office because his landlord wanted to do construction. And so this manager was between offices having a terrible run and also, you know, I knew a lot of his other investors and about half the capital left that year. And so I'm sitting down with this manager at what we ended up being our local spot. We'd sit there for 2 or 3 hours, talk to the portfolio, what's working, what's not working, how he's behaving. And it it gave me confidence to to not quit. In hindsight, I really wish we would have added more, but um in those moments, you feel challenged and you feel paralyzed sometimes. But what I could tell myself is that I believed in the underlying holdings. This manager had, for lack of a better way to say it, no quit in him. And it was clear that even though the AUM was challenged, the business was not. And he had this really interesting line that he kept saying to us. And he kept saying, you know, I really want to earn back your capital. I want to reward you for your patience. And it was a very different dynamic because what normally happens is assets down, performance poor. managers start this whole trope of, okay, well, I'm gonna shut down and start a family office. And and that's okay to each their own. But this manager was going the exact opposite. And so we hung in there. And it doesn't always work out this way. But things started to turn in 2021. And from 21 to 26, it's been a 5x. So he's compounded significantly over this period. And so it turned into can we stay? we should have added to just constantly trimming this manager to manage a position overall. And what was really funny in that whole rhetoric of I want to reward you for your patience. I hope to reward you for your patience is he started going down a different path of saying, you know, someday I've become my capital in the fund continues to compound and you know, I'd like to get it over 50% and someday, you know, I just really think I should just have a family office. And so as we've been trimming, there's been this really interesting dynamic where we would trim x amount and he would call kind of two days before that redemption was going to happen and he'd say, "Hey, do you mind if I send uh $2 million more dollars?" And he was really just doing the math for himself and then he was looking and constantly is looking at, "Hey, what do I want to own versus my AUM?" And if I want to own things that my AUM doesn't work for, I'm just going to send money back. So, he is totally a rare bird, but that is an instance where we got rewarded for for being patient. You suggested that the underlying holdings were good, the business was good. What was it in addition to those things that gave you the confidence to see it through with this manager? >> In my role, I'm always going to have a view on the securities held. But I also have to balance that with my role is to pick managers and to trust them and to check my own bias. If we just had a portfolio of things that I thought were good, we wouldn't end up with a diversified portfolio. So when I zoomed out on this manager, because of his skill on the short side, because of the types of holdings he had, I was able to zoom out and say, you know what, this has been painful, but we don't have anything else like this in our portfolio. This is a hedge if things get a lot worse in certain areas. Because you probably remember 2020 and 2021 were these really euphoric years and then all of a sudden we had 2022 and in a lot of ways this manager's long book recovered in 21 and then the short book just thrived in 2022. And so the message to the team cuz as you get further away from that manager, the team has more and more questions and it's reasonable. That's it's there to check your bias. And so they just look at the numbers and go this is terrible. Why do we have this? And so it wasn't just that I believed in the manager. They weren't going to quit and I liked what they own. It was they served a distinct role in the portfolio and that was worth fighting for. When the team has questions and even beyond that, when the board has questions about an underperforming manager for a substantial period of time, how do you effectively communicate that message to both the team and the board to stay patient? It's difficult and uh I don't want to leave the impression that I do it perfectly nor do we always make the right choice. Um, but I start with generally once you're already in that period of underperformance, that draw down, certainly things can get a lot worse. But if you can limit some of the the left tails, you probably experience most of the pain. And so then it's what's next? And at the team level, it's a lot of meetings. It's a lot of conversations. And an example like this, we will often bring in a colleague. And so one of my favorite colleagues on the team joined me in a lot of these trips down to Dallas at the restaurant and lost Kolenas and she was a great check on my bias and that also helped build towards a consensus on the team to be patient. Fortunately, we have a board that is less focused on that kind of volatility at the line item level level. They certainly care and they certainly ask, but we've been able to build a relationship where there's real patience there. And so it all worked together in this example to allow us to survive the path. And kind of where we started is that's why relationships matter because you have to prepare for those moments because when you're there, you you never know when they're going to happen and you certainly rarely can predict them. So that groundwork was laid with our stakeholders and our board. So, that's a fun example where the outcome was favorable for everybody. How about an example where a manager went through a tough time and they weren't as transparent and they were more guarded. >> So, we've certainly had those two. And I I think there's this weird phenomenon that if I had to lay the course of a business, it would be for a PM to have their rough patch or one of their rough patches early in the fund's life. hopefully not in the first year, but in within a relative short period of time because there's so much learning about oneself and and a team in those moments. And the example I'd speak to is someone who had just a great 10-year run and then things started to falter and they hadn't really built that muscle or prepared for that moment. And so instead of sharing more with their LPs, they actually started to to to tense up and and really not share. And then they ended up actually doing some nonLP friendly actions in terms of liquidity and otherwise. And it just it made that moment that we were just speaking about in terms of how do you survive the path with a team not tenable because at the moment that the institutional base, the team, the investor starts to doubt the relationship, the patience just disappears. And so so in that case it was a we need to move on from this relationship because there's not the ability to survive that path. The interesting thing here is the manager is still at it and you know I think they have a great chance of performing in the future but with a very different investor base and I'm and I'm not exactly sure they've learned the right lessons from that experience for themselves but again each person has to figure it out for themselves and everyone has their own path. >> Transparency clearly is an important driver in your investment philosophy. Do you have any examples of a time when manager was too transparent? >> Yeah. Um I think it's really hard to normalize for transparency and of course every situation's different. And so we had a manager we worked with for a long time put together a great track record. Um it's a long only shop and he he let us know that one of his employees had departed. And what you normally get is some kind of story and that's it. and that's the industry and and that's okay. we should, you know, protect individuals mostly. But he felt compelled to tell us what happened. And effectively, without getting into too many details, the analyst lied to him and he called him out on it. And then he lied to him again. And he basically said, "At that point, you can't work here anymore because we don't lie. We don't lie to each other. That's not what we do." And so we had this conversation with all the details and and of course he was embarrassed to share the story and he wasn't happy because he invested in this person, time, money, everything. And he really believed in this person. But what he showed us was really his compass around call it ethics or just the right way to be drove everything he did. But I still remember getting off the that call and turning to my colleague and and she asked me, you know, is is he just not good at managing people? Is he does he have a deficiency? And and then we had to just keep talking about it and we said no no actually he's just more transparent because we are it's framed to us that all these firms are perfect and professional the way we want them to be. But inside of them are humans and humans are complicated and a window into that actually gives us more empathy and understanding and support for this manager long term. But it was a real risk to share that much with us and so we ultimately appreciate it. But um it's a fine line to balance. >> How long does it take to establish that strong relationship with a manager? >> Everyone's different. I generally think we want to know people long enough so that we're ready to commit to them and stick stand by them. But there is a great manager that we work with that really only has a handful of investors, manages a meaningful amount of money for them. And he really prioritizes relationships more than I've really seen anyone else. He's a very unique manager in that way. And so he has this annual meeting and I spent about six years getting to know him and really enjoyed all the conversations. And over that period, his returns were just extraordinary. And so you watch those returns and you say, "Wow, I wish I was in this fund." But more importantly, you get to know the person and you say, "This is just a really great person. This is the kind of person I want to spend time with. This is this is really great. He's a really great investor." And so he has this annual meeting. It's it's a really fun get together. It's like a mini Birkshire in some ways. And uh you know, it's over the weekend. you travel to get there. And so I went for my second year. I've now I think gone seven or eight. And uh we still weren't invested and it was certainly on my mind. And he is very capital conscious and really only likes to take capital when there's a dislocation or idiosyncratic opportunity in his portfolio. And I still remember, of course, he's spending time with lots of people, but I run into him and we're having um a conversation and I say, "Do you think we should talk about um potentially partnering?" And he says, "Don't you think we should get to know each other better more?" And then he says, "Don't you think we should spend more time getting to know each other?" and and I, you know, I I so appreciate that moment now, but I was so focused on investing and he was very focused on what I've learned matters the most. So, I really appreciate it. >> Love that. One of the things that comes to mind from these examples is the potential bias for smaller managers given that they just might be more transparent than larger managers. And I'm curious if that's true, if that comes to fruition as one of your potential biases given the emphasis on transparency. >> It's a real bias and we try to address it and we work to really try to be diversified on the age of firm, the size of firm and really not to look away from large funds because some of those funds are absolutely great and where you want to be. And so there's a specific example with um a very well-known spin out of another very very well-known fund that um I sat down with this individual when they were launching and I said, "We really like to have relationships. I don't think we could ever really have the relationship that would work for me, but I find what you do really compelling and and it could serve a great role in our portfolio." And so it was really interesting because somehow that seemed to resonate with him and uh he took the challenge on and so you know they sit at over 30 billion in assets today. But certainly then and it's it's it's evolved as he's had more demands on his time. But then he he took the challenge full hog and we had phone calls, we were texting, we it just was a very different relationship than I expected from someone in that seat. And what the firm's done as it's grown is figure out a way to keep that kind of connectivity and transparency. And it's never just, hey, I need to know what you own at all times. It's help me understand what you're doing so that I can tell I can tell that message so I can understand performance so that so that I can be your advocate and I can also know when to lean in. And so this specific firm has found a way to navigate that path. And it's especially hard because a fund of this size is is often in the press. They have to be careful and guarded about what they own, but they've come up with pretty unique ways about getting LPs together, be it, you know, group dinners or otherwise. And then they've built out just a really robust team of IRBd individuals that really fill in that gap. And so because we started at one place, I still feel that there's this connection with this individual. And it's not about my feelings in this relationship, just to be clear. I don't need to be uh everyone's best friend, but it's simply how do you know enough to understand the individual, what they're doing. And then that leads into understanding when they start to stray from that path from from kind of who you thought they were, when they start to flail. >> You mentioned an interesting point, which is when you engage with investor relations. This is one example of a firm that you suggest does this quite well. What are some other examples that you have of managers effectively utilizing investor relations for relationships with you? We have a great COO PM team that's really produced great results and the COO started as the IRBD person and you know earlier I spoke to this idea of when a manager has a rough patch really shapes the way that they I think view themselves and how they talk to LPs and and often I think how they interact with their team. And so this individual spun out of of fund that we had a relationship with over time and we backed him at launch and he was a manager who didn't have any kind of acute underperformance at inception or in the first few years but really just had a string of subpar years and it was mostly due to his cyclical focus. He was in sectors that were out of favor and so the first five years had this really muted return. I mean mid single digits when the market was really chugging along at high single low double but he was generally outperforming his sectors and security selection was there and and and another trait that I really really like that's so rare is he's just a great shorterter this fund uh has made money since inception shorting and they're in their 11th year but because of that period in the beginning where the firm was tested it was tested in a bunch of ways they lost a partner early on who um really challenged the PM's I everything from identity to choices and and the the now COO really was critical in becoming everything from a product specialist to managing relationships in a way that everyone understands what's going on. And so yes, that's being transparent about holdings, but it's also say it's a short book and you don't want to be talking about each specific short. It's making sure you communicate that at a level like uh say themes or sectors or anything so that someone can understand and support it and mostly because of his personality but I also think because of how he was tested early he listens to her and they challenge each other and I would argue they come to the relationship as equals even though he's the PM and it's his firm and so they set a culture of doing things that are prettycept exceptional. It's a public hedge fund cross cap structure but mostly equities and they have an annual meeting and at that annual meeting every one of the analysts spends time with investors and so you end up just spending the day in groups of five six people rotating rooms and you're just by yourself with the analyst that's covering oil and gas or the analyst that's doing something else and there's no script, there's no prompt. They just show that they trust their people. They're not trying to over script it because they're trying to be authentic to who they are. And so I still remember again this firm had this really meh period of returns for the first 5 years. And I remember a phone call I got from a friend who was looking at these returns and said why are you in this fund? and and and you know I was getting similar push from our team and it was a fair question and again it was we don't have a lot of exposure to this area and there could be a day and when it works and I see this manager getting better every year every day and their mentality is such that all they want to do is improve and so along that same time I still remember it was the depth of co and this is also where their performance turned that another friend of mine asked asked me to have a phone call with with their CIO to convince them not to fully redeem out of this manager. So, it was one of those really testable moments, but the the crazy thing is that you look at the fund now and everyone says, "Oh, well, you know, they're just a great firm." The run since 2020 has been a 6x and so the returns have been great, but it's because they were battle tested early and built a business there and they haven't lost that culture. >> It's a remarkable example and I'm curious what your advice would be to funds that don't approach investor relations in that manner. Look, every firm is different and they have different needs and so I don't ever want to prescribe one path but a lot of it seems to start with egos and yes the investment team the PM is driving the P&L of that firm but it's more than that because it's about communicating and so when you view your IRBD as an ancillary function you're missing a really big resource. ource that is your front line, that is your time saver, that is so many other things for your firm. And so I think it's very hard for anyone to check their ego and really just try to be a partner with everyone at their firm, but if you can get every resource on your team optimized, it really pays. >> This has been a fascinating dive into a lot of the tenants of your investment philosophy. I'd love to turn to discuss your process to find out more about how you approach finding new managers and keeping and maintaining relationships with managers. And so to start, sourcing, you know, sourcing is every LP's bread and butter and critical to your job. What's your perspective on sourcing? >> It's critical. I think it's super important, but it really has to start with what are the resources of the team. And I think too often some of the large organizations are looked to in the same way that a small organization is thinks it should behave. And so if you don't have the resources, you can't meet every manager and you have to be careful with your time. And so I say all that only to say that much of our industry is focused on finding the best managers always and then hoping for the best in terms of what it does in creating a portfolio. And everybody wants the best managers, but I think more than half of the role in sitting at an institution and investing its capital is being a portfolio manager, is optimizing the team you have and working every day to make sure your money weighted return is better than your time weighted return. It sounds so easy to add to what's not working and subtract from what is, but it's certainly very very hard. And so sourcing is being willing to be out there. Sourcing is using your network to meet great people, but it's also being willing to take a cold call. But at the same time, you can't take all the cold calls cuz you don't have that much time in the day. And so it feels a lot like hit and miss. But when someone stands out, it's usually because they're exceptional in a way that seems so basic. And it's just that they are themselves. The authenticity comes through the the the curiosity with investing, the fascination with investing. And then it starts to become okay, they're a great investor, but what role do they serve in our portfolio? And it's having discipline around saying, you know what, we already have exposure to biotech. How much biotech should we have? We can't just only own biotech. And so there's different sections of a portfolio. You're going to be more benchmark aware in your public book than you're going to be maybe in your hedge fund book. But that being said, it needs to be constructed for diversification. You need to have portfolio effects that are meaningful. And so sourcing absolutely matters, but it's just one piece of the puzzle. Once you've determined the portfolio fit, you've determined you need to pursue this particular area. How do you then structure your time effectively to uncover all of the rocks that you could uncover in that particular area? In our seat, we're constantly being challenged with requests for our time when a lot of times having space to think and do desk work would probably be more valuable. That said, when there is someone you want to partner with or think you might want to partner with, there's no amount of time that isn't well spent. Because when you start to think about 5 to 10 year plus relationships you really want to spend time with these individuals as humans and so it's balancing that time. It is a battle for everyone that does this job because it is very easy to go chase a shiny thing and you have to know when is enough. The best investors we work with can take a 100red data points and tell you the one to three that they think matter. So that's a skill that we're always trying to hone and work on. You absolutely need to do a comp set analysis. You absolutely need to know what else is out there. But I'm not sure there's a lot of value in looking at the 30th fund that does the same thing that you're looking at as as potentially your investment. And it's really hard to say that to say there might be an indifference point here. And so then that goes back to portfolio construction and how that matters so much. So different managers end up giving you unique betas and those betas are very important but how they work together is your responsibility not theirs. And of course, you're always looking for the the idiosyncratic alpha, etc. However, it's still got a huge beta component. So, you need it all to work together. And that's where kind of the art and science come together of of all this work because everything you do quantitatively is backward-looking. And it's very important and it informs decisions. But you you really need a sense of where you think that manager might go and what that does to your aggregate portfolio. Once you've determined fit in the portfolio, you've honed in on a particular manager, how then do you make decisions on making an allocation or not? >> There's an institutional momentum that starts to be its own type of inertia and being able to fight that is very important. There was a PM that I used to run Central Park with and he had a big well-known endowment. spending a lot of time with him. And on one of our runs, I just said, "Hey, how many hours have you spent with this organization?" And he said, "Oh, like 70." And I said, "Oh, they're going to invest." And that's not always the case, but it's there's this correlation of time. And so I don't say that to say that that's the way it should be. I say that because that's just this bias that we always have to check. You need to be willing to to to to turn away at any point if for some reason it doesn't fit the portfolio the way you thought. they not they they aren't who you think they were. For us internally, we have a pretty tight process of how things bubble up into our internal IC. Those conversations are robust uh lots of different perspectives and that's that's the design. And you want to be challenged before you go into the investment because you need to be able to make the investment such that if that manager has that rough patch that we've talked about in the first year of your investment or the first three years of your investment, you understand why and you're hopefully there to stick through it and maybe even add. But you know, every case is different. But if you don't have that conviction from the start, you shouldn't make the investment. So, you're a generalist with an emphasis on public markets and and hedge funds. I'm curious how you approach sourcing and decision-m across public and private market investments. The difference between public and private is private is a lot less reversible. And the irony is that private funds generally don't allow you the same access that publics do. and they generally show up and say, "Hey, we're closing in a month. Are you in? Are you out for the next 10 to 15 years?" And so the [clears throat] strategy there is to be spending time with managers when they're not raising to to really request as much time as possible so that you feel okay with that relationship. Some of our best private relationships come through really spending that time for for the same reasons that applied on the public side. The big difference is you don't have all this noise. You don't have Mr. Market, you know, in in in all these erratic moments being euphoric and then depressed. And so there's less decision points. So a 5% position and a five basis point position can often get the same amount of airtime at an internal investment committee meeting. And and that's not necessarily the way it should be. And it's certainly something that we continue to always try to frame. So when you just say dollars, you probably should also say percent. And so we try to always not just say, hey, it's x million. It's you say so many basis points. And that just reframes things. All these things can matter. And a 10x on a small position can be just as meaningful as a slowly or meaningfully compounding big position. But balancing that is hard. >> You're invested with the manager. You're maintaining relationships. You've talked about the importance of transparency. Let's talk about the situation where the relationship and performance has soured and you need to exit. How does that happen? In hindsight, we exit too late in most cases because when something turns and doesn't improve, you look back and say, "Wow, we should have left earlier." I gave some examples of where we were rewarded for patience. The main difference if there is one is that there need to be a few factors to have patience and that's stability of business, grit in the manager to keep going and a belief in what they own. And really also inside of that is watching for style drift, watching for erratic behavior. All kinds of things that are telltel signs of of someone truly doubting themselves. And if you've ever experienced a 30 or 50% draw down, you'd be insane not to doubt yourself. So it's it's balancing that is is real. And so we certainly do part ways with managers, but we try to do it in the best way possible. And so um I can think of a story of a manager that um has since shut down. And uh you know in hindsight he started to kind of flail in terms of what he was doing with his portfolio. Statistically you could see that the turnover was increasing. Our conversations really led to uncovering a real self-doubt that was creeping into everything he was doing. And so we felt the fund would shut down. We felt it was time to leave. This is someone that I'd gotten to know for four or five years. and felt was just a really great human and really great investor truly despite the the period of of really rough luck. So this manager happened to be in Singapore and so I had other reasons to be there but flew out to Singapore let him know what we were doing in person face to face. It was the kind of way I'd like to do this side of the business because ultimately you have to recognize how difficult that is in their seat. We still have a good relationship. And the irony in most of these situations is what that manager owned in today's market would be doing incredibly well. Style drift, analysis of the underlying holdings, erratic behavior. What are some of the other tenants that you consider exiting from a manager position? Certainly any improper behavior is just you're done. Um so obviously anything illegal is is is game over. But really there's a lot of gray area that when the partnership starts to be not prioritized, it's there's there's just better things to do with your capital. There's also plenty of times you move on from a manager because the portfolio has different needs and uh potentially there was a time where a manager A did something and manager B did something different and now both managers are doing something similar and you have to make a choice and that's okay too. But I think the hardest thing to to really understand in these periods of stress is if the manager has the grit to keep going. And these are often well-off individuals and sometimes they just decide it's not worth it. And so that distance to high watermark matters, the direction of aum matters, all these things matter. But the reality is you need to be consistent. You need to say, are we the type of team that's going to generally, not always, every strategy is different, but favor mean reversion, or are we going to try to be a momentum shop, but when you start to go between the two, you really set yourself up to always miss the bus stop, if you will. So, I don't think anybody could get 10 out of 10 calls when a manager is having a tough time, right? But if we can get six to seven to eight of those calls right, I think we end up being rewarded significantly because even if you got six out of 10, those six will probably have much more meaningful returns on the upside than the four that uh petered out from there because usually by the time you're at that point, you're already somewhere towards the trough of that draw down. You mentioned a tight decision-making process with the team and robust dialogue that happens with the board when it comes to allocation decisions. How's that process differ when exiting a relationship? >> At Trinity, we have internal discretion, but we certainly started with with working with our board on every decision and and the goal there was to make sure they understood what we did and what we're doing and to have real support over time. The first thing that was passed on to not need approval was exit and trim decisions. And the logic there is simple. Those generally if you lose faith, you should be out. And so those generally happen faster. When a manager is struggling, we will do what we call a formal review. And these happen periodically regardless, but they get pulled forward if there's a moment of pain. And this is a way to get everyone on the same page in something, you know, in pros effectively with of course a bunch of data in there to to understand what's going on. And you know, you basically have three choices. Sell, hold, buy. But it's great when the team is really challenging and saying, "Hey, look, if we're not adding here, why are we holding this?" And the opposite is when something's working, we should maybe trim so that if it stops working, we could add. But needless to say, exit decisions generally need to happen faster because they always fill gradually than all of a sudden. And when that happens, you already wish you'd already made the decision one quarter, year earlier. >> Something that you've already mentioned is the importance of portfolio management in addition to the manager selection process. I'm curious how your relationship forward transparent approach to manager selection ultimately informs the portfolio management and asset allocation of your book. >> Where we benefit from the level of transparency we've worked very hard to get with our managers is we have a much better sense of say daily P&L. Now in our seat you should do nothing with that because you need to stay longterm. However, that also allows for a much better sense of where exposures are, be it geographic sector or otherwise, on a mid-month basis. And when we have very volatile months like we've experienced recently, it's very nice to know where you are in real time, not where you were at last month's end. And so that changed the dynamic around portfolio management, but it doesn't really change the end goal. And it's simply what are the aggregate risks? And we need to be making bottom up manager selection decisions that build to top to tilts and then we have to manage those tilts because our managers have no idea what the aggregate portfolio looks like. And so specifically in a long only portfolio when you're much more benchmark aware, it's really managing the aggregate tilts in a way that allows you to not be out of bounds with benchmark in a way that wouldn't be long-term advisable. Because the thing you definitely want to check is people in our seat making too many top- down calls because we do not have the tools to do that. nor are we generally the experts in those spaces. >> How do you effectively manage those tilts especially in a market environment today with so much concentration especially in public public equity markets? >> Public equities have been specifically a tough place to be. Managing tilts has everything to do with starting with a risk framework around what would we accept in terms of over underweight the US over underweight something and you need to do that in reference to your benchmark of course going from there it's also knowing the toolkit and the speed at which we can operate and so it starts with portfolio construction because we we're not making generally a lot of mid-month adjustments to exposures. It's it's really a long-term goal and and it's a moving target. And so it starts with a discipline around hey do we really need to add exposure to country X right now because we already are overweight that benchmark and then it becomes hey we have 2x the aque exposure to country Y we need to be trimming there and so it's just on the margin but it really matters over time because investing is hard and benchmarks have been especially especially hard in in in recent years because of the concentration. So So then it's then it's also you're under overweight to the top holdings of the benchmark and thinking about how you want to address that. Would love to turn Nick to get your thoughts on today's market environment and one area in particular that is top of mind for all LPs is liquidity. Curious how you and Trinity approach the liquidity dynamic with exit. so slow on the private side and your focus is on the public markets. >> We're in an okay position because we started building our private portfolio in 2016. So, we have certainly a meaningful private allocation, but it's not as illquid as as some liquidity across the industry has been rough. Liquidity doesn't seem to have a clear path in a lot of cases going forward. Certainly, there's some large IPOs coming through. That'll be its own interesting dynamic that uh with record size will be something to watch and learn from. But in many cases, it's slowed down the deployment of additional capital into privates. But it's also changed the dynamic of the conversation. And you pair that with a public market that's vastly outperformed the broad private market measured on say a Cambridge index level. And it's causing most people I know to have very difficult conversations around what is the future path of private private allocation in our portfolio. And then the contrarian says at what point is it going to be a great time? At what point are we going to see some really amazing vintages because of where the market's going? The other side of that is have too many companies waited too long to go public. And so that's what's going to be really interesting with really the next few quarters and and and the the market dynamic we see and and and how the public market digests IPO volume 2x uh anything we've seen in history. >> What is your answer to what private market allocations role are in an institutional portfolio? It's hard not to be skeptical at this point because private markets have, and this is dangerous to generalize, but gotten a pass in a way that the public markets don't. And being someone who lives more public, you deal with the day-to-day and you just envy the private seat that says be patient. So we do discuss and say that privates are not essential meaning we should choose to have them because of the return they we believe they will deliver over time and that return needs to be in excess of what's offered in public markets. It can be a diversifier as well, but it needs to be a real diversifier and not just because of uh quarterly marks or some other kind of laundering of capital. And so we are really questioning the path forward, but we will certainly have private exposure in the future and we're very excited about the partners we have. And so it's really hard to look at the private market that's struggling to develop a private market that's struggling to produce the liquidity that investors are hoping to see and a public market near or at all-time highs. You would think now is the time. So then you start to go down this path of if you can't sell now, what does that say about marks on a broad basis? and and really what did we do to ourselves in 20 and 21 because that was this euphoric time in the private markets where funds came back to the market instead of at the third year maybe at the 12th month and so I don't have an answer in terms of what comes next but it's certainly not binary it's cautious and at some point there'll be a great opportunity to partner with groups that maybe have a little less demand than they're used to and also partner with with new funds that are going about it the right way. >> Can you believe it that we've made it over an hour and have not mentioned the words AI? >> I'm so proud of us. >> All right, Nick, this has been a lot of fun. So, appreciate all of the anecdotes that you've been able to share. Want to turn to some closing questions before we let you go. >> What is your favorite hobby or activity outside of work and family? I'm working to become what's called a master mason and that's in the dry stone wall category. It has a uh a fourlevel track. I'm preparing for my second level test and it feels like uh one of these baking shows because you have to build an component of a wall by 8 hours and you're being judged as it goes and you know it's it's it's like when the cooking shows when they say there's 30 minutes or 10 minutes left and everyone starts running. Um anyway, uh I have fallen in love with Stacking Stones. >> Amazing. What was your first paid job and what did you learn from it? >> So, I was an art model. It turns out that you could work in the cafeteria or you could make double if you were the art model for your peers in art class. And apparently, I thought that was the economic choice. And so, I learned that um that can be embarrassing, but it's character building. And I also learned that standing still or striking opposed for extended periods of time is very hard for me. >> Next one. What is your biggest investment pet peeve? >> We as an industry have a lot of expressions and I find some of them to be really hard and I would love us to find new ones. Whenever there's an executive summary, I'm always wondering if there's a regular summary or if a consultant just added a word. Um I don't believe that any baby has ever been thrown out with a bathwater. And then one I think we really should stop using is decimate. It literally means down 10%. Uh it was referring to Roman soldiers losing their lives and so it was emotional and so maybe emotional decimation but it just doesn't work when you say a stock was decimated because that's a price movement and I think 10% in my head. >> I don't know who we have to talk to about any of these but we'll write a letter to someone. All right, Nick. Last one. What advice would you give to a young allocator that you wish you knew earlier in your career? >> You're never going to know, so it's okay not to know. Meaning, the more comfortable you can get with not knowing the answer and being okay with that there is no answer, the better investor you can become. It doesn't mean you don't have to do a bunch of work to try to understand what it is you're doing, but to think that there's a spreadsheet or a co-pilot answer is just not a thing. >> Nick, thank you so much for doing this. loved our conversation. >> Thank you for having me. [music]
